sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Electrical injuries occur when electrical current passes through the body, causing tissue damage. Injuries are often classified as high voltage (>1,000 volts) or low voltage (<1,000 volts). Severity depends on voltage, current pathway, duration of contact, and associated trauma. Injuries may result from direct contact, electrical arc, lightning, or flame/flash burns. Alternating current is often more hazardous than direct current because it can cause sustained muscle contraction and arrhythmias.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Pain
- Burns, entry and exit wounds
- Respiratory arrest or dysfunction
- Cardiac arrest, arrhythmias, palpitations, myocardial damage
- Urinary symptoms
- Neurological deficits, ALOC, seizures, amnesia, motor dysfunction, dysphasia, spinal cord damage
- Fractures – consider spinal injuries
- Compartment syndrome (more likely from high voltage exposure)
- Visual and auditory disturbances

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Confirm the source is de-energised before approach
- High-voltage injuries may cause deep tissue damage, dysrhythmia, compartment syndrome, and occult trauma
- Associated trauma from falls or blast effects should be actively sought
- Low-voltage exposure with a normal assessment is often lower risk, but loss of consciousness, chest symptoms, or ECG abnormality requires more caution

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Do not approach until the source is confirmed de-energised
- Primary Survey with c-spine consideration where indicated
- Support airway and ventilation and administer oxygen if required
- Assess burns, entry and exit wounds, fractures, and muscle injury
- Record vital signs and complete focused secondary survey
- Manage burns under the burns guideline and transport if high-risk features are present

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Obtain 12 lead ECG and cardiac monitoring where indicated
- Treat arrhythmias under the relevant guideline
- Gain IV access and provide fluids or analgesia only when clinically indicated
- Escalate for high-voltage injury, significant burns, loss of consciousness, cardiac symptoms, or traumatic arrest

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- AC, and high DC currents can cause muscular spasm/contraction in which the individual is unable to let go of the electrical source, further, exposure can cause the individual to be hurled away from the electrical source. Household electrical sources are AC
- Lightning injuries: May present with asystole and fixed pupils; aggressive resuscitation is warranted as survival rates are higher than other causes of cardiac arrest
